Gender Representation in BBC Music’s Hip-Hop Poll

Author

Harper Eskey

1 GitHub

https://github.com/harpereskey/musicproject

2 Introduction

This project looks at data from BBC Music’s hip-hop poll, where more than 100 critics, artists, and music industry people from 15 countries each picked their five favorite hip-hop tracks. The main question is: does artist gender affect how songs are nominated and ranked?

The BBC used a point system to score the results:

“We awarded 10 points for first ranked track, eight points for second ranked track, and so on down to two points for fifth place. Songs with more votes ranked higher in the case of a tie; any remaining ties were split by first-place votes, followed by second-place votes.” — BBC Music

This project uses summary statistics, charts, a model, and trend analysis to look at whether the poll just reflects a male-dominated industry, or whether there is also bias in how critics rank female artists.

3 Data Description

Two datasets are used in this analysis.

polls — one row per critic nomination:

Variable Description
rank Rank given by the critic (1 to 5)
title Song title
artist Artist name
gender Gender of artist or group
year Year the song came out
critic_name Name of the critic
critic_rols Critic’s job or role
critic_country Critic’s main country
critic_country2 Critic’s secondary country

rankings — one row per song with total poll results:

Variable Description
ID Song ID
title Song title
artist Artist name
year Year released
gender Gender of artist or group
points Total points earned
n Total votes received
n1 to n5 Votes at each rank position

4 Load Data

library(readr)
polls <- read_csv("data/polls.csv")
ranks <- read_csv("data/rankings.csv")

5 Summary Statistics

5.1 Average Rank by Artist Gender

The table below shows the average rank given to songs by artist gender. A lower number means critics put those songs higher on their list.

library(dplyr)

polls |>
  group_by(gender) |>
  summarise(
    n_nominations = n(),
    avg_rank = round(mean(rank), 2)
  ) |>
  arrange(avg_rank)
# A tibble: 3 × 3
  gender n_nominations avg_rank
  <chr>          <int>    <dbl>
1 male             470     2.95
2 mixed             26     3.15
3 female            39     3.54

Female artists get fewer nominations overall, and when they are nominated, their average rank is worse than male artists. This raises the main question: is the gap because of how the music industry is made up, or is there also bias in how critics rank female artists?

5.2 Rank Distribution by Gender

This table shows what share of nominations each gender got at each rank position (1 = best, 5 = worst). This shows not just whether female artists are nominated, but where they tend to land on critics’ lists.

ranks |>
  group_by(gender) |>
  summarise(
    ratio_n1 = round(sum(n1) / sum(n), 3),
    ratio_n2 = round(sum(n2) / sum(n), 3),
    ratio_n3 = round(sum(n3) / sum(n), 3),
    ratio_n4 = round(sum(n4) / sum(n), 3),
    ratio_n5 = round(sum(n5) / sum(n), 3)
  )
# A tibble: 3 × 6
  gender ratio_n1 ratio_n2 ratio_n3 ratio_n4 ratio_n5
  <chr>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 female    0.077    0.103    0.308    0.231    0.282
2 male      0.215    0.204    0.194    0.194    0.194
3 mixed     0.115    0.269    0.154    0.269    0.192

6 Critics Analysis

6.1 Female Nominations per Critic

This looks at how many female artists each critic included in their five picks.

polls2 <- polls |>
  group_by(critic_name) |>
  summarise(
    n_female = sum(gender == "female", na.rm = TRUE),
    ratio_women_top5 = round(mean(gender == "female", na.rm = TRUE), 3),
    ratio_men_top5 = round(mean(gender == "male", na.rm = TRUE), 3)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(ratio_women_top5))

head(polls2, 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 4
   critic_name      n_female ratio_women_top5 ratio_men_top5
   <chr>               <int>            <dbl>          <dbl>
 1 Kirsten Zemke           3              0.6            0  
 2 Fabian Wolff            2              0.4            0.4
 3 Kathy Iandoli           2              0.4            0.4
 4 Kish Lal                2              0.4            0.6
 5 Yi-Ling Liu             2              0.4            0.6
 6 Antoinette Isama        1              0.2            0.6
 7 Biba Adams              1              0.2            0.6
 8 Brother Ernie           1              0.2            0.8
 9 Camille Storm           1              0.2            0.6
10 Common                  1              0.2            0.8

Most critics nominated zero or one female artist out of five picks. This means underrepresentation is spread across many critics, not just a few outliers.

7 Visualizations

7.1 Votes by Gender and Rank Position

This chart shows the total number of nominations by artist gender, with each bar colored by rank position.

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(data = polls,
       aes(x = gender, fill = factor(rank))) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Rank") +
  labs(
    title = "Count of Top-Five Votes by Artist Gender",
    x = "Artist Gender",
    y = "Number of Nominations"
  )

Male artists have far more nominations. The spread of rank positions looks similar across genders, so the main gap is in how often female artists are nominated, not necessarily where they land when they are.

7.2 Nominations for Female Artists by Critic Country

Do critics from some countries nominate female artists more than others? The red dashed line shows the overall average.

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

country_gender <- polls |>
  filter(!is.na(critic_country), !is.na(gender)) |>
  group_by(critic_country) |>
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    ratio_female = mean(gender == "female", na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  filter(total >= 5) |>
  arrange(desc(ratio_female))

ggplot(country_gender,
       aes(x = reorder(critic_country, ratio_female),
           y = ratio_female, fill = ratio_female)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mean(country_gender$ratio_female),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Prop. Female") +
  labs(
    title = "Proportion of Female Artist Nominations by Critic Country",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = overall average | Countries with < 5 nominations excluded",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Proportion of votes for female artists"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

New Zealand critics nominate female artists at a much higher rate than average. This fits with New Zealand’s history of gender equality, including being the first country to give women the right to vote in 1893 and having close to 50% female representation in parliament. Critics from the US and UK, who make up most of the poll, tend to fall at or below the average.

7.3 Female Artist Nominations by Critic Role

Does a critic’s job affect whether they nominate female artists?

role_female <- polls |>
  filter(!is.na(critic_rols), !is.na(gender)) |>
  group_by(critic_rols) |>
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    ratio_female = mean(gender == "female", na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  filter(total >= 5, ratio_female > 0)

ggplot(role_female,
       aes(x = reorder(critic_rols, ratio_female),
           y = ratio_female, fill = ratio_female)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mean(role_female$ratio_female),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Prop. Female") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Female Artist Nominations by Critic Role",
       x = NULL, y = "Proportion of female artist nominations")

There is real variation across roles. Some roles, like artists themselves, nominate female artists at higher rates. This suggests that who gets included as a critic in the poll also shapes the results.

8 Predictive Model: Does Artist Gender Affect Song Rank?

To test whether artist gender predicts rank, a simple linear regression is fit with rank as the outcome and gender as the predictor.

polls <- polls |>
  mutate(gender = factor(gender))

model1 <- lm(rank ~ gender, data = polls)
summary(model1)

Call:
lm(formula = rank ~ gender, data = polls)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-2.53846 -0.94681  0.05319  1.05319  2.05319 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)   3.5385     0.2257  15.679   <2e-16 ***
gendermale   -0.5917     0.2349  -2.519   0.0121 *  
gendermixed  -0.3846     0.3568  -1.078   0.2816    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 1.409 on 532 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.01239,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.008673 
F-statistic: 3.336 on 2 and 532 DF,  p-value: 0.03633

8.1 Model Visualization

library(tidyverse)
library(ggeffects)

pred <- ggpredict(model1, terms = c("gender")) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  as_tibble()

ggplot(data = polls, aes(x = gender, y = rank)) +
  geom_point(aes(fill = gender),
             position = position_jitter(width = 0.25, height = 0.12),
             alpha = 0.3, shape = 21) +
  geom_point(data = pred,
             aes(x = x, y = predicted, fill = x),
             size = 5, shape = 21) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Does Artist Gender Affect Song Ranking?",
    subtitle = "Small points = individual nominations; large points = model-predicted mean",
    x = "Artist Gender",
    y = "Rank (1 = Best)"
  )

The model shows a small but consistent difference: female artists get an average rank of about 3.5, compared to 2.9 for male artists. Female artists are nominated less often and ranked lower when they are nominated. The mixed group sits in the middle, meaning groups with female members are not penalized as much as solo female artists.

9 Trends Over Time

9.1 Songs by Artist Gender Over Time

Has the number of female artist nominations changed over the years the poll covers (1979 to 2019)?

year_gender <- polls |>
  group_by(year, gender) |>
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop")
ggplot(year_gender, aes(x = year, y = count, color = gender)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_viridis_d(name = "Gender") +
  labs(
    title = "Trends in Nominations by Artist Gender Over Time",
    x = "Year of Song Release",
    y = "Number of Nominations"
  )

Male artists lead nominations in every decade, which roughly matches the hip-hop industry being about 70 to 80% male. 1989 was the first year a mixed-gender group showed up in a critic’s top five. There is a spike in female nominations in the mid-1990s, around 1992 to 1998. This was when artists like Queen Latifah, Missy Elliott, and Lauryn Hill were breaking through, even though this was one of the most male-dominated eras in hip-hop. The trend line for female nominations is mostly flat, meaning there has not been much increase in representation over time.

10 Interactive Visualization

This Shiny app lets you filter nominations by year range and group them by country or gender.

library(shiny)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Hip-Hop Poll Explorer"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      sliderInput("year", "Year Range",
                  min = 1979, max = 2019,
                  value = c(1979, 2019), sep = ""),
      radioButtons("variable_choose",
                   label = "Group nominations by:",
                   choices = c("critic_country", "gender"))
    ),
    mainPanel(
      plotOutput("plot")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  filtered <- reactive({
    polls |>
      filter(year >= input$year[1], year <= input$year[2])
  })

  output$plot <- renderPlot({
    ggplot(filtered(),
           aes(x = .data[[input$variable_choose]], y = rank, color = gender)) +
      geom_jitter(alpha = 0.4, width = 0.25, height = 0.1) +
      scale_color_viridis_d(name = "Gender") +
      theme_minimal() +
      labs(title = paste("Nominations by", input$variable_choose),
           x = input$variable_choose,
           y = "Rank (1 = Best)") +
      theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

11 Discussion

11.1 Bias vs. Representation

The main challenge with this data is separating two possible explanations for the gender gap.

The first is representation. Hip-hop has always been a male-dominated genre. If critics are nominating songs they genuinely love, a mostly male list might just reflect what music is out there, not bias.

The second is ranking bias. Even when female artists are nominated, they tend to get ranked lower than male artists. The average rank for female artists is about 3.5, compared to 2.9 for male artists. This gap is harder to explain as just reflecting the industry.

Both explanations are probably true at the same time. And underneath both is a bigger question: why are there so few female hip-hop artists to begin with? If female artists face more barriers to making and releasing music, the poll’s gender gap may start long before critics ever write down their lists.

11.2 Key Findings

Female artists make up a small share of total nominations. When they are nominated, they tend to be ranked lower than male artists. Critics from New Zealand nominate female artists at higher rates than average. A critic’s job also predicts how likely they are to nominate female artists. The mid-1990s had a clear spike in female nominations that does not appear in other decades. Overall, female representation in the poll has not grown much over time.

12 Reflection

The data shows two things happening at once: female artists are nominated less often, and when they are nominated, they tend to be ranked lower. Both of these point to gender shaping how critics engage with hip-hop.

But it is hard to separate personal taste from industry structure. Critics are picking from a pool of music that is already mostly made by men. At the same time, the ranking gap among nominated artists suggests something beyond just reflecting the industry.

The most honest takeaway is that the poll’s gender imbalance likely comes from multiple places at once: who gets to make music, who gets recognized, and who comes to mind when critics are asked to make a list. This data alone cannot fully answer that question, but it shows the pattern clearly.

13 References

13.1 Data Sources

Tidy Tuesday (2020). BBC Music Hip-Hop Poll Dataset. Retrieved from https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday

BBC Music (2019). The Greatest Hip-Hop Songs of All Time. British Broadcasting Corporation.

13.2 AI Assistance (Claude)

The following prompts were submitted to Claude (Anthropic) during this project. Responses helped with code and background context, but all analysis decisions were made by the author.

# Prompt Purpose What was used
1 (code + debug) Fixing visualization code Debugging Added factor(rank) to aes(fill = ...) in the votes-by-gender bar chart
2 “Here is what I have, what else can I explore?” Ideation Suggestion to use critic_rols, which led to the critic role visualization
3 “From 1979 to 2019, how many male vs. female music artists were there?” Background context Estimate that roughly 70 to 80% of popular music artists were male over this period
4 “What was going on in music from 1992 to 1998?” Background context Context about the era being male-dominated despite notable female breakthroughs
5 Provided country_gender code and asked for plot suggestions Visualization Suggestion to add a geom_hline() reference line; author changed color from blue to red

13.3 Google Searches

The following searches were done to add real-world context to patterns in the data.

# Query Finding used
1 “Is New Zealand more progressive with women’s rights?” New Zealand was the first country to give women the right to vote (1893) and has had close to 50% female parliamentary representation
2 “Is New Zealand more progressive with women’s rights in 1990 to 2000?” New Zealand was highly progressive in this period, with major political milestones and stronger legal protections for women
